bonusReads.title: How ChatGPT Was Actually Trained: The RLHF Story
bonusReads.preview: GPT-3 could generate text — but also said terrible things. Here's the three-stage training that turned a raw language model into a product used by 100 million people.

sections.heading: THE GPT-3 PROBLEM
sections.text: GPT-3 was trained to predict the next token on internet text — which means it also learned from toxic content. Ask it a question and it might continue the question rather than answering, because on the internet, questions are often followed by more questions.

sections.heading: THREE STAGES OF RLHF
steps: SFT: Human labellers wrote ideal responses to thousands of prompts. GPT-3 was fine-tuned on these.
Reward Model: Labellers ranked multiple model responses. A separate model learned to predict these rankings.
PPO: The SFT model is further trained using the reward model as feedback. Higher-scoring responses get reinforced.

sections.heading: THE ALIGNMENT TAX
sections.callout: RLHF makes models more helpful — but often reduces raw benchmark performance. OpenAI called this the alignment tax.
sections.text: InstructGPT scored lower on some benchmarks than raw GPT-3 — but humans vastly preferred it. Benchmark performance and product usefulness are different things.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) achieves similar alignment without the RL training loop — just a clever loss function on preference pairs. Most modern fine-tuning uses DPO or variants. When asked 'how do you align a model?' — this is your answer.

bonusReads.title: Constitutional AI: How Anthropic Built Claude Without Human Feedback on Harmful Content
bonusReads.preview: RLHF requires humans to rate harmful outputs. Constitutional AI makes the model critique its own harmful outputs using a written constitution — no labellers needed.

sections.heading: THE PROBLEM
sections.text: To train a safe model via RLHF, you need humans to evaluate harmful content. Hiring labellers to read thousands of toxic responses at scale is expensive and harmful to the workers.

sections.heading: THE APPROACH
steps: Write a Constitution: principles the AI should follow.
SL-CAI: The model critiques and revises its own harmful outputs using the constitution. Fine-tune on the revised outputs.
RLAIF: An AI model scores outputs using the constitution instead of human labellers. Use these scores for RL training.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: Constitutional AI is why Claude exists. The key insight: LLMs are capable enough to critique themselves and generate preference data. RLAIF is now standard thinking at Anthropic and Google DeepMind. When asked 'how do you align an LLM without RLHF?' — this is your answer.

bonusReads.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning: Meta's Analysis of When Each Wins
bonusReads.preview: Meta ran a systematic comparison of RAG vs fine-tuning vs both combined. The results clarified when each approach actually works — and the answer isn't what most people assume.

sections.heading: THE RESULTS
steps: Knowledge updates frequently: RAG wins. Fine-tuning freezes knowledge. RAG updates the moment you update the vector store.
Tone and format changes (always answer in JSON): fine-tuning wins. RAG cannot reliably change output style.
Deep domain reasoning (legal, medical): fine-tuning + RAG wins.
Cost: RAG is ~10x cheaper than fine-tuning at scale.

sections.heading: THE DECISION FRAMEWORK
sections.callout: Use RAG when the knowledge changes. Use fine-tuning when the behaviour changes. Use both when you need deep domain reasoning over live data.
sections.text: This is the single most important AI product decision you'll make repeatedly. Every senior AI engineering interview will ask it. Know the four dimensions: knowledge freshness, behaviour vs knowledge, cost, and latency.

bonusReads.title: How Uber's Two-Tower Neural Net Matches Riders to Drivers in 2 Seconds
bonusReads.preview: Every Uber match is a real-time ML ranking problem. Two-tower neural networks, ANN search, and feature stores — the same architecture that powers RAG retrieval.

sections.heading: THE ARCHITECTURE
steps: Rider request fires — Feature Store returns pre-computed features.
Two-tower net: one tower encodes rider context, one encodes each candidate driver as embeddings.
Dot product similarity ranks candidates — top-K go to a reranker.
Reranker applies business constraints: driver earnings fairness, surge cap.

sections.heading: WHY THIS MATTERS FOR AI ENGINEERS
sections.text: Two-tower models are the industry standard for large-scale retrieval. One tower = query, one tower = documents. Fast dot product at scale = Approximate Nearest Neighbour search. This is exactly how your RAG vector search works. Same architecture, different domain.

bonusReads.title: How Notion AI Ships: Streaming, Context Management, and Knowing When Not to Use AI
bonusReads.preview: The most important metric for AI writing isn't accuracy — it's time-to-first-token. Notion obsessed over showing the first word in under 300ms.

sections.heading: THE UX INSIGHT
sections.text: If the user sees the first word in 300ms, the AI feels fast. If they wait 2 seconds for the first word, it feels broken — even if total response time is the same. Notion's streaming architecture was built around this perception.

sections.heading: HOW THEY STREAM
steps: User clicks 'Improve Writing' — frontend shows loading state immediately.
Backend fires streaming API call — as soon as the first token arrives, it's sent via SSE.
React renders each token as it arrives — fills in word-by-word.
Crucially: the full page is NOT sent to GPT — only selected block + surrounding context (< 2000 tokens).

sections.heading: WHEN NOT TO USE AI
sections.callout: The hardest product decision was choosing what NOT to make AI. Spell check, bullet formatting, basic grammar — deterministic rules are cheaper, faster, and more reliable.
sections.text: Knowing when NOT to invoke an LLM is a core AI engineering judgment. A regex is cheaper, faster, and more reliable than a 200ms LLM call for structured operations.

bonusReads.title: How Google Scaled PaLM to 540B Parameters Across 6,144 TPUs
bonusReads.preview: Training a 540B parameter model requires splitting it across thousands of chips. The engineering behind PaLM is as impressive as the model itself.

sections.heading: THREE TYPES OF PARALLELISM
steps: Tensor Parallelism: single matrix multiplications split across chips. Each computes a slice, all-reduce combines. High communication overhead.
Pipeline Parallelism: model layers split into stages across chip groups. Bubble overhead between microbatches.
Data Parallelism: same model processes multiple microbatches in parallel. Gradient sync cost.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: At Google, Meta, or DeepMind: know what each parallelism type splits (tensors, layers, data) and the tradeoffs. You don't need to implement them — you need to explain them clearly and compare their costs.
